content: $$ A \text { and } B \text { respectively are } $$

8.8 g of monohydric alcohol added to ethyl magnesium iodide in ether liberates $2240 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of ethane at STP. This monohydric alcohol when oxidised using pyridinium-chloro-chromate, forms a carbonyl compound that answers silver mirror test (Tollen's test). The monohydric alcohol is
explanation: Given that, 8.8 g of monohydric alcohol liberates $2240 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of ethane. So, 88 g of monohydric alcohol will liberate $22400 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ (STP) Now, the carbonyl compound that can oxidise Tollens reagent is aldehyde and it is the oxidising product of $1^{\circ}$ alcohol. So, among the given options 2, 2-dimethyl propan-1-ol (molecular mass $=88$ ) is the required monohydric alcohol.

explanation:

Thus, the IUPAC name of the product B is 2-methyl propene.

content: PCC is

options.content: A complex of chromium trioxide with pyridine $+\mathrm{HCl}$

explanation:
PCC is pyridinium chlorochromate. It is a complex of chromium trioxide with pyridine +HCl , i.e. $\left[\mathrm{C}_5 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{NH}\right]^{+}\left[\mathrm{CrO}_3 \mathrm{Cl}\right]^{-}$.

It is a reagent in organic synthesis used primarily for oxidation of alcohols to form carbonyl.

content: A better reagent to oxidise primary alcohols into aldehyde is
explanation: PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) is a commonly used reagent for the oxidation of primary alcohols to aldehyde. It is favoured due to its selectivity, mild reaction conditions, compatibility with functional groups and ease of handling.

content: The major product obtained when ethanol is heated with excess of conc. $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$$ at $$443 \mathrm{~K}$$ is
explanation:
The major product obtained when ethanol is heated with excess of conc. $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$$ at $$443 \mathrm{~K}$$ is ethene. The reaction involve is as follows

$$\underset{\text { Ethanol }}{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{OH}} \xrightarrow[443 \mathrm{~K}]{\text { Conc. } \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4} \underset{\text { Ethene }}{\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}_2}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$

content: Among the following, the products formed by the reaction of anisole with $$\mathrm{HI}$$ are
explanation: Phenol and iodomethane are formed by the reaction of anisole with $$\mathrm{HI}$$.

content: Which is most VISCOUS?
explanation:
We know that, higher is the extent of $$\mathrm{H}$$-bonding higher will be the viscosity.

Among the given option glycerol has the highest viscosity as in this case $$3$$-$$\mathrm{OH}$$ groups are involved in hydrogen bonding which is greater than other ethylene glycol has two and both ethanol and methanol has one $$-\mathrm{OH}$$ group.

content:
Which of the following on heating gives an ether as major products?

(P) $$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Br}+\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{ONa}$$

(Q) $$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{ONa}+\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Br}$$

(R) $$\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{ONa}$$

(S) $$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCl}+\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{ONa}$$
explanation:
Here, $$P$$ and $$Q$$ both on heating will produce ether as major product.

$$\underset{\substack{\text { (1 halide) } \\ \text { Benzyl bromide }}}{\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Br}}+\underset{\substack{\text { Sodium } \\ \text { methoxide }}}{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{ONa}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OCH}_2 \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5+\mathrm{NaBr}$$

$$\underset{\substack{\text { Sodium } \\ \text { phenoxide }}}{\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{ONa}}+\underset{\text { (1 halide) }}{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Br}} \longrightarrow \underset{\begin{array}{c} \text { Methoxy benzene } \\ \text { (Anisole) } \end{array}}{\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OCH}_3}+\mathrm{NaBr}$$

Here, aryl halide and sodium alkanide cannot be used for preparing phenolic ethers because of very less reactivity of aryl halides.

content:
Match the following acids with their $$pKa$$ values :

Acid $$pKa$$
A. Phenol i. 16
B. $$p$$-Nitrophenol ii. 0.78
C. Ethanol iii. 10
D. Picric acid iv. 7.1

options.content: A - iii, B - iv, C - i, D - ii
A - ii, B - i, C - ii, D - iv
A - iii, B - i, C - iv, D - ii
A - iv, B - ii, C - iii, D - i

explanation:
Key Idea Acidic strength is inversely proportional to the $$p \mathrm{~K}_a$$ value. The correct match is

$$\mathrm{a} \rightarrow \mathrm{iii}, \mathrm{b} \rightarrow \mathrm{iv}, \mathrm{c} \rightarrow \mathrm{i}, \mathrm{d} \rightarrow \mathrm{ii}$$

Phenols are stronger acids than alcohols. The ionisation of an alcohol and a phenol takes place as follows

In alkoxide ion, the negative charge is localised on oxygen while in phenoxide ion, the charge is delocalised. The delocalisation of negative charge makes phenoxide ion more stable and favours the ionisation of phenol. In substituted phenols, the presence of electron withdrawing groups such as nitro group enhances the acidic strength of phenol. The stronger the acid, the lower the $$p \mathrm{K}_a$$ value.

content: Which of the following can be used to test the acidic nature of ethanol?
explanation:
The reactions of ethanol with metals (e.g., sodium) indicate its acidic nature. It reacts with active metals such as sodium to yield corresponding alkoxide and hydrogen

$$\mathrm{2R - OH + 2Na \to \mathop {2R - \mathop O\limits^ - - \mathop N\limits^ + a}\limits_{Sodium\,alkoxide} + {H_2}}$$

The acidic character of alcohols is due to the polar nature of $$\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{H}$$ bond. These are weaker acids than water.

content: Phenol can be distinguished from ethanol by the reagent
explanation:
(a) Bromine water is used to distinguish phenol from ethanol, as phenol give white ppt. of 2, 4, 6 tribromophenol with $\mathrm{Br}_2$ (aqueous).

    While, ethanol will not give any such reaction.

content:
What is the increasing order of acidic strength among the following?

    (i) p-methoxy phenol

    (ii) $p$-methyl phenol

    (iii) $p$-nitro phenol
explanation: In substituted phenol, presence of electron withdrawing group. Such as $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ group increases the acidic strength of phenol but presence of electron releasing group such as alkyl and alkoxy group decreases the acidic strength of phenol $-\mathrm{OCH}_3$ group is more electron releasing group then $\mathrm{CH}_3$ so, $p$-methoxy phenol is less acidic than $p$-methyl phenol.

content: Which of the following reagent cannot be used to oxidise primary alcohols to aldehydes?

options.content: Heating in presence of Cu at 573 K

explanation:
$\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ in acidic medium cannot be used to oxidise primary alcohols to aldehydes because it convert primary alcohols into carboxylic acid the direct oxidation of primary alcohols to carboxylic acids can be carried out using $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$.

$$ \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}^{+}]{\mathrm{KMnO}_4} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH} $$

content: The product formed during the following reaction are
Which of the following order is true regarding the acidic nature of phenol?
explanation: Presence of electron releasing group like $\mathrm{CH}_3$ over benzene nucleus destabilises the phenoxide ion, thus decreases the acidity of phenol where as presence of electron withdrawing group like $\mathrm{NO}_2$ stabilises the phonoxide ion and thus increases the acidity of phenol, hence order of acidity will be O-cresol < phenol $<\mathrm{O}$ - nitrophenol
